title: EU AI Office publishes frontier-AI expert findings: two years to fix compute, capital, and copyright
subtitle: A hundred-expert panel told the Commission that Europe has a narrow window to compete on frontier AI — and that the barriers are structural, not scientific.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: The European Union's AI Office published a report on July 15 summarizing findings from more than 100 outside experts on how the bloc can strengthen its competitiveness, sovereignty, and security in frontier AI. The panel's headline conclusion was that Europe has roughly one to two years — not five — to move to a position of strength before the frontier consolidates around a handful of non-EU labs. The most urgent priorities named are computing infrastructure and the energy to power it. The report also names structural drags: growth-stage capital, legal uncertainty around training-data copyright and data protection, and talent attraction and retention.

The report is not itself binding, but it lands with unusual timing. The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2, high-risk system rules pick up on the same date next year, and the Commission is simultaneously running its July action plan on cybersecurity and AI — including a call to expand the bloc's independent AI-model evaluation capacity by 2027. The expert findings are effectively the technical brief the Commission will lean on when it argues that regulation alone will not close the gap: Europe needs private capital, gigawatts of power, and a copyright regime that does not push frontier training to other jurisdictions.

The subtext is the competitive position of Mistral, Aleph Alpha, and a small number of other EU labs relative to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Meta. The panel's recommendation to selectively back 'high-risk, high-return bets' on alternative approaches and other layers of the AI stack reads as an argument for state-backed frontier scale-ups, not just startup grants. Whether Brussels can move that fast is the political question — the report itself flags that structural barriers, not scientific ones, are the limit.

A note for learners: when you see a jurisdiction publish a report like this, read the priorities as a live map of what is going to get funded or regulated in the next twelve months. 'Compute and energy' at the top of the list is not just a technical observation — it means data-center permitting and grid connections will get political attention, which changes where builds actually happen. If you are considering a career adjacent to AI policy, or a job at a lab in Europe, this is the document that tells you where the leverage points are for the rest of 2026.
